pattern: Bit swaps let every element except the all-ones value be paired into a 1 and a (2^p-2), so the minimum product is (2^p-1) times (2^p-2) raised to 2^(p-1)-1, taken under the modulus.
hints: Swapping bits conserves the multiset of bits, so you may reshape all numbers in [1, 2^p-1] freely as long as none becomes zero.
Pairing x with (2^p-1-x) turns each pair into a 1 and a (2^p-2); the lone all-ones number stays untouched.
There are 2^(p-1)-1 such pairs, giving the product (2^p-1) * (2^p-2)^(2^(p-1)-1); compute it with modular exponentiation under 1e9+7 but using the true exponent.

stress.description: every valid p so the closed form is checked across the whole domain
stress.note: small finite domain; random p over the full range

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def minNonZeroProduct(self, p):
        MOD = 10**9 + 7
        if p == 1:
            return 1
        mx = (1 << p) - 1
        base = (mx - 1) % MOD
        exp = (1 << (p - 1)) - 1
        # Independent iterative binary exponentiation (no built-in pow).
        result = 1
        b = base
        e = exp
        while e > 0:
            if e & 1:
                result = (result * b) % MOD
            b = (b * b) % MOD
            e >>= 1
        return (mx % MOD) * result % MOD
